sections.heading: Application of Act
sections.title: Application of Act to Data Processing
sections.summary: This section defines when the Data Protection Act applies to personal data processing. It specifies that the Act binds the State, treats each government department as separate, applies to automated and non-automated processing where data forms part of a filing system, and excludes exchanges between government entities on a need-to-know basis and purely personal data processing activities. It also establishes criteria for when entities outside Mauritius are covered by the Act.

requirements.text: This Act shall bind the State
Each Ministry or Government department shall be treated as separate from any other Ministry or Government department

This Act shall apply to the processing of personal data, wholly or partly, by automated means and to any processing otherwise than by automated means where the personal data form part of a filing system or are intended to form part of a filing system
This Act shall not apply to the exchange of information between Ministries, Government departments and public sector agencies where such exchange is required on a need-to-know basis
This Act shall not apply to the processing of personal data by an individual in the course of a purely personal or household activity
This Act shall apply to a controller or processor who is established in Mauritius and processes personal data in the context of that establishment

This Act shall apply to a controller or processor who is not established in Mauritius but uses equipment in Mauritius for processing personal data, other than for the purpose of transit through Mauritius
Every controller or processor referred to in subsection (5)(b) shall nominate a representative established in Mauritius

For the purpose of subsection (5)(a), any person who is ordinarily resident in Mauritius or carries out data processing operations through an office, branch or agency in Mauritius shall be treated as being established in Mauritius

sections.source_quote: 3. Application of Act (1) This Act shall bind the State. (2) For the purposes of this Act, each Ministry or Government department shall be treated as separate from any other Ministry or Government department. (3) This Act shall apply to the processing of personal data, wholly or partly, by automated means and to any processing otherwise than by automated means where the personal data form part of a filing system or are intended to form part of a filing system. (4) This Act shall not apply to – (a) the exchange of information between Ministries, Government departments and public sector agencies where such exchange is required on a need-to-know basis; (b) the processing of personal data by an individual in the course of a purely personal or household activity. (5) Subject to section 44, this Act shall apply to a controller or processor who – (a) is established in Mauritius and processes personal data in the context of that establishment; and (b) is not established in Mauritius but uses equipment in Mauritius for processing personal data, other than for the purpose of transit through Mauritius.

sections.heading: Principles relating to processing of personal data
sections.title: Principles for lawful processing of personal data
sections.summary: Controllers must ensure personal data processing complies with six principles: lawfulness and transparency, explicit legitimate purposes, relevance and minimization, accuracy and timeliness, limited retention, and respect for data subject rights.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical for DTIA as it defines core data processing principles that must be adhered to across all data handling activities, including cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: Personal data must be processed lawfully, fairly and transparently
Personal data must be collected for explicit, specified and legitimate purposes
Personal data must be adequate, relevant and limited to necessary purposes
Personal data must be accurate and kept up to date with timely corrections
Personal data must be kept for no longer than necessary for processing purposes
Personal data must be processed in accordance with data subject rights

sections.source_quote: Every controller or processor shall ensure that personal data are – (a) processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to any data subject; (b) collected for explicit, specified and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner incompatible with those purposes; (c) adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed; (d) accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date, with every reasonable step being taken to ensure that any inaccurate personal data are erased or rectified without delay; (e) kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; and (f) processed in accordance with the rights of data subjects.

sections.heading: Collection of personal data
sections.title: Rules for collecting personal data from data subjects
sections.summary: Controllers may not collect personal data unless it serves a lawful purpose and is necessary for that purpose. Direct collection requires informing data subjects of specific details including purpose, recipients, consent status, rights, and data retention periods, with exceptions for pre-existing information or legal requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical for DTIA as it details requirements for data collection, including transparency and consent mechanisms that impact cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Controller may not collect personal data unless it serves a lawful purpose connected with controller's function
Controller may not collect personal data unless it is necessary for that purpose

Controller must inform data subject of identity, contact details, purpose, recipients, consent status, withdrawal rights, data subject rights, automated decisions, retention period, complaint rights, and cross-border transfers
Controller must not be required to comply with subsection (2) if data subject already has the information
Controller must not be required to comply with subsection (2) if data collection is impossible or disproportionate effort
Controller must not be required to comply with subsection (2) if data disclosure is legally mandated

sections.source_quote: Subject to section 44, a controller shall not collect personal data unless – (a) it is done for a lawful purpose connected with a function or activity of the controller; and (b) the collection of the data is necessary for that purpose. Subject to subsection (3), where a controller collects personal data directly from a data subject, the controller shall, at the time of collecting the personal data, ensure that the data subject concerned is informed of – (a) the identity and contact details of the controller and, where applicable, its representative and any data protection officer; (b) the purpose for which the data are being collected; (c) the intended recipients of the data; (d) whether or not the supply of the data by that data subject is voluntary or mandatory; (e) the existence of the right to withdraw consent at any time, without affecting the lawfulness of processing based on consent before its withdrawal; (f) the existence of the right to request from the controller access to and rectification, restriction or erasure of personal data concerning the data subject or to object to the processing; (g) the existence of automated decision making, including profiling, and information about the logic involved, as well as the significance and the envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject; (h) the period for which the personal data shall be stored; (i) the right to lodge a complaint with the Commissioner; (j) where applicable, that the controller intends to transfer personal data to another country and on the level of suitable protection afforded by that country; and (k) any further information necessary to guarantee fair processing in respect of the data subject's personal data, having regard to the specific circumstances in which the data are collected.

sections.heading: Notification of personal data breach
sections.title: Notification of personal data breach to Commissioner
sections.summary: Controllers must notify the Commissioner within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach, including details on affected data subjects and records. Processors must notify controllers without undue delay. Notifications must describe the breach, provide contact details for the data protection officer, and recommend remedial actions. Controllers must specify facts to enable verification of compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely breach notification to the Commissioner with specific details, impacting DTIA by mandating breach reporting timelines and content for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Controller must notify Commissioner within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach
Controller must provide reasons for delay if notification is late
Processor must notify controller without undue delay upon becoming aware of breach
Notification must describe nature of breach, affected data subjects and records
Notification must include contact details of data protection officer
Notification must recommend measures to address breach and mitigate adverse effects
Controller must specify facts to enable Commissioner verification of compliance

sections.source_quote: 25. Notification of personal data breach (1) (a) In the case of a personal data breach, the controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the personal data breach to the Commissioner.

sections.heading: Communication of personal data breach to data subject
sections.title: Communication of personal data breach to data subject
sections.summary: Controllers must inform data subjects of a breach likely to cause high risk to their rights if they have not already done so, after notifying the Commissioner. Communications must be in clear language and include details from section 25. Exceptions include when technical measures like encryption prevent breach impact, subsequent measures mitigate risk, or public communication is equally effective.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines when and how data subjects must be notified of breaches, impacting DTIA by requiring breach communication protocols for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Controller must communicate breach to data subject without undue delay if breach likely to cause high risk to rights
Communication must describe breach in clear language and include information from section 25
No communication required if controller has implemented encryption or other technical measures
No communication required if subsequent measures mitigate high risk
No communication required if disproportionate effort and public communication is equally effective
Commissioner may require communication if controller has not already done so

sections.source_quote: 26. Communication of personal data breach to data subject (1) Subject to subsection (3), where a personal data breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of a data subject, the controller shall, after the notification referred to in section 25, communicate the personal data breach to the data subject without undue delay.

sections.heading: Duty to destroy personal data
sections.title: Duty to destroy personal data
sections.summary: Controllers must destroy personal data as soon as reasonably practicable when the purpose for keeping it lapses, and notify processors holding the data. Processors must destroy specified data as soon as reasonably practicable upon receiving such notification.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires data destruction upon purpose lapse, impacting DTIA by mandating deletion timelines and notification for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Controller must destroy personal data as soon as reasonably practicable when purpose lapses
Controller must notify processors holding the data
Processor must destroy specified data as soon as reasonably practicable

sections.source_quote: 27. Duty to destroy personal data (1) Where the purpose for keeping personal data has lapsed, every controller shall – (a) destroy the data as soon as is reasonably practicable; and (b) notify any processor holding the data.

sections.heading: Special categories of personal data
sections.title: Special categories of personal data processing
sections.summary: Special categories of personal data may only be processed if consent is given, processing is necessary for legal claims, health care, or specific rights, or the data subject is incapable of consent. Processing must be conducted by not-for-profit bodies with safeguards and limited disclosure.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts processing of special categories, impacting DTIA by requiring specific safeguards for sensitive data transfers.

requirements.text: Special categories may only be processed if section 28 applies
Processing must be carried out by not-for-profit bodies with political, philosophical, religious or trade union aims
Processing must relate solely to members or former members of the body
Processing must not disclose data outside the body without consent
Processing may be necessary for legal claims
Processing may be necessary for health care, medical diagnosis, treatment or social care

sections.source_quote: 29. Special categories of personal data (1) Special categories of personal data shall not be processed unless – (a) section 28 applies to the processing; and (b) the processing is carried out in the course of its legitimate activities with appropriate safeguards by a foundation, association or any other not-for-profit body with a political, philosophical, religious or trade union aim and on condition that the processing relates solely to the members or to former members of the body or to persons who have regular contact with it in connection with its purposes and that the personal data are not disclosed outside that body without the consent of the data subjects; (c) the processing relates to personal data which are manifestly made public by the data subject; or (d) the processing is necessary for – (i) the establishment, exercise or defence of a legal claim; (ii) the purpose of preventive or occupational medicine, for the assessment of the working capacity of an employee, medical diagnosis, the provision of health or social care or treatment or the management of health or social care systems and services or pursuant to a contract with a health professional and subject to the conditions and safeguards referred to in subsection (2); (iii) the purpose of carrying out the obligations and exercising specific rights of the controller or of the data subject; or (iv) protecting the vital interests of the data subject or of another person where the data subject is physically or legally incapable of giving consent.

sections.heading: Personal data of child
sections.title: Personal data of child below 16 years
sections.summary: Personal data of children below 16 years may only be processed with consent from parents or guardians. Controllers must verify consent through reasonable efforts considering available technology.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires parental consent for children under 16, impacting DTIA by mandating consent verification for cross-border data transfers involving minors.

requirements.text: Personal data of children below 16 years may only be processed with consent from parent or guardian
Controller must verify consent through reasonable efforts considering available technology

sections.source_quote: 30. Personal data of child (1) No person shall process the personal data of a child below the age of 16 years unless consent is given by the child's parent or guardian.

sections.heading: Data protection impact assessment
sections.title: Data protection impact assessment for high-risk processing
sections.summary: Controllers and processors must conduct data protection impact assessments before processing operations that pose high risks to data subjects' rights and freedoms, such as large-scale profiling, special category data processing, or systematic monitoring. The assessment must evaluate necessity, proportionality, risks, and risk-mitigation measures, including safeguards and security protocols.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates DTIAs for high-risk processing, ensuring adequate safeguards for cross-border data transfers and sensitive data handling.

requirements.text: Controllers and processors must conduct data protection impact assessments before high-risk processing operations
High-risk processing includes systematic and extensive automated profiling with legal effects
High-risk processing includes large-scale processing of special categories of data
High-risk processing includes systematic large-scale monitoring of publicly accessible areas
High-risk processing includes other operations requiring consultation with the Office
Assessments must describe envisaged processing operations and purposes
Assessments must evaluate necessity and proportionality of processing operations
Assessments must evaluate risks to data subjects' rights and freedoms
Assessments must include measures to address risks and safeguards for data protection

sections.source_quote: Where processing operations are likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of data subjects by virtue of their nature, scope, context and purposes, every controller or processor shall, prior to the processing, carry out an assessment of the impact of the envisaged processing operations on the protection of personal data.

sections.heading: Prior authorisation and consultation
sections.title: Prior authorisation and consultation for data processing
sections.summary: Controllers and processors must obtain prior authorisation from the Commissioner before processing personal data, especially when transfers to other countries are involved. They must also consult the Commissioner if data protection impact assessments indicate high risks or if the Commissioner deems consultation necessary. The Commissioner can prohibit non-compliant processing and require remedial actions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires prior authorisation for cross-border data transfers, ensuring DTIAs meet regulatory standards and mitigate risks for international data flows.

requirements.text: Controllers and processors must obtain authorisation from the Commissioner before processing personal data
Controllers and processors must consult the Commissioner before processing personal data if data protection impact assessments indicate high risks
Controllers and processors must consult the Commissioner before processing personal data if the Commissioner deems consultation necessary
The Commissioner may prohibit processing if it does not comply with the Act
The Commissioner may require remedial actions for non-compliant processing

sections.source_quote: Every controller or processor shall obtain authorisation from the Office prior to processing personal data in order to ensure compliance of the intended processing with this Act and in particular to mitigate the risks involved for the data subjects where a controller or processor cannot provide for the appropriate safeguards referred to in section 36 in relation to the transfer of personal data to another country.

sections.heading: Transfer of personal data outside Mauritius
sections.title: Transfer of personal data outside Mauritius
sections.summary: Personal data may be transferred outside Mauritius if the Commissioner is satisfied with appropriate safeguards, data subjects provide explicit consent after being informed of risks, transfers are necessary for contractual or legal purposes, or transfers occur from public registers. The Commissioner may prohibit or condition transfers to protect data subjects' rights.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines conditions for cross-border data transfers, requiring safeguards, consent, or legal necessity, which are critical for DTIAs involving international data flows.

requirements.text: Transfers may occur if the Commissioner is satisfied with appropriate safeguards for data protection
Transfers may occur if data subjects provide explicit consent after being informed of transfer risks
Transfers may occur if necessary for contractual performance or pre-contractual measures
Transfers may occur if necessary for legal claims or public interest
Transfers may occur if necessary to protect vital interests of data subjects
Transfers may occur if legitimate interests are not overridden and safeguards are proven
Transfers from public registers must not involve entire data sets
The Commissioner may request proof of safeguards or compelling legitimate interests
The Commissioner may prohibit, suspend or condition transfers to protect data subjects' rights

sections.source_quote: A controller or processor may transfer personal data to another country where – (a) he or it has provided to the Commissioner proof of appropriate safeguards with respect to the protection of the personal data;

sections.heading: Unlawful disclosure of personal data
sections.title: Unlawful disclosure of personal data
sections.summary: Controllers and processors who disclose personal data without lawful excuse commit offences. Unauthorized access to personal data and disclosure to others also constitute offences, except for employees acting within their mandate. Offering to sell personal data obtained unlawfully is also prohibited.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibits unlawful disclosure of personal data, impacting cross-border data transfers through data security and confidentiality requirements.

requirements.text: Controllers who disclose personal data without lawful excuse commit offences
Processors who disclose personal data without controller's prior authority commit offences
Unauthorized access to personal data and disclosure to others commit offences
Employees acting within mandate are exempt from offences
Offering to sell personal data obtained unlawfully commits offences

sections.source_quote: Any controller who, without lawful excuse, discloses personal data in any manner that is incompatible with the purpose for which such data has been collected shall commit an offence.

sections.heading: Commencement
sections.title: Commencement date for the Act
sections.summary: This section specifies the commencement date of the Act, stating that it comes into operation on a date to be fixed by Proclamation, with the possibility of different dates for different sections. The Act was proclaimed by a specific Proclamation in 2018, effective from 15 January 2018.

requirements.text: This Act shall come into operation on a date to be fixed by Proclamation.
Different dates may be fixed for the coming into operation of different sections of this Act.

sections.source_quote: 58. Commencement Proclaimed by [Proclamation of 2018] w.e.f. 15 January 2018 (1) Subject to subsection (2), this Act shall come into operation on a date to be fixed by Proclamation. (2) Different dates may be fixed for the coming into operation of different sections of this Act.

sections.heading: Short title
sections.title: Short title of Data Protection Act 2017
sections.summary: This section defines the short title of the Act as the Data Protection Act 2017, providing a clear reference for legal documents and proceedings. It establishes the official name of the legislation governing data protection in Mauritius.

requirements.text: This Act may be cited as the Data Protection Act 2017

sections.source_quote: 1. Short title This Act may be cited as the Data Protection Act 2017.

sections.heading: Interpretation
sections.title: Definitions for Data Protection Act 2017
sections.summary: This section provides comprehensive definitions for key terms used throughout the Data Protection Act 2017, including 'personal data', 'data subject', 'controller', 'processor', and 'data breach'. These definitions establish the legal framework for data processing activities and accountability.

requirements.text: "authorised officer" means an officer to whom the Commissioner has delegated his powers under section 13
"biometric data" means any personal data relating to the physical, physiological or behavioural characteristics of an individual which allow his unique identification
"collect" does not include receive unsolicited information
"Commissioner" means the Data Protection Commissioner referred to in section 4
"consent" means any freely given specific, informed and unambiguous indication of the wishes of a data subject
"controller" means a person who or public body which, alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes and means of the processing of personal data
"data subject" means an identified or identifiable individual
"document" includes – (a) a disc, tape or other device in which information other than visual images are embodied
"encryption" means the process of transforming data into coded form
"filing system" means a structured set of personal data which is accessible according to specific criteria
"genetic data" means personal data relating to the general characteristics of an individual which are inherited or acquired
"physical or mental health" includes information on the provision of health care services to the individual
"individual" means a living individual
"information and communication network" means a network for the transmission of messages
"Minister" means the Minister to whom responsibility for the subject of data protection is assigned
"network" means a communication transmission system that provides interconnection among a number of local and remote devices
"Office" means the Data Protection Office referred to in section 4
"personal data" means any information relating to a data subject
"personal data breach" means a breach of security leading to the accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to, personal data
"proceedings" means any proceedings conducted by or under the supervision of a Judge or Magistrate
"processor" means a person who, or public body which, processes personal data on behalf of a controller
"processing" means an operation or set of operations performed on personal data
"profiling" means any form of automated processing of personal data consisting of the use of personal data to evaluate certain personal aspects relating to an individual
"pseudonymisation" means the processing of personal data in such a manner that the personal data can no longer be attributed to a specific data subject
"recipient" means a person to whom, or a public body to which, personal data are disclosed
"register" means the register referred to in section 20
"registration certificate" means the registration certificate referred to in section 16 (2)
"restriction of processing" means the marking of stored personal data with the aim of limiting their processing in the future
"special categories of personal data" means personal data pertaining to racial or ethnic origin
"telecommunication network" means a system, or a series of systems, operating within such boundaries as may be prescribed
"third party" means a person or public body other than a data subject, a controller, a processor
"traffic data" means any data relating to a communication by means of a computer system
"Tribunal" means the ICT Appeal Tribunal set up under section 35 of the Information and Communication Technologies Act

sections.source_quote: 2. Interpretation In this Act – "authorised officer" means an officer to whom the Commissioner has delegated his powers under section 13; "biometric data" means any personal data relating to the physical, physiological or behavioural characteristics of an individual which allow his unique identification, including facial images or dactyloscopic data; "collect" does not include receive unsolicited information; "Commissioner" means the Data Protection Commissioner referred to in section 4; "consent" means any freely given specific, informed and unambiguous indication of the wishes of a data subject, either by a statement or a clear affirmative action, by which he signifies his agreement to personal data relating to him being processed; "controller" means a person who or public body which, alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes and means of the processing of personal data and has decision making power with respect to the processing; "data subject" means an identified or identifiable individual, in particular by reference to an identifier such as a name, an identification number, location data, an online identifier or to one or more factors specific to the physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural or social identity of that individual; "document" includes – (a) a disc, tape or other device in which information other than visual images are embodied so as to be capable, with or without the aid of some other equipment, of being reproduced from the disc, tape or other device; and (b) a film, tape or other device in which visual images are embodied so as to be capable, with or without the aid of some other equipment, of being reproduced from the film, tape or other device; "encryption" means the process of transforming data into coded form; "filing system" means a structured set of personal data which is accessible according to specific criteria, whether centralised, decentralised or dispersed on a functional or geographical basis; "genetic data" means personal data relating to the general characteristics of an individual which are inherited or acquired and which provide unique information about the physiology or health of the individual and which result, in particular, from an analysis of a biological sample from the individual in question; "physical or mental health", in relation to personal data, includes information on the provision of health care services to the individual, which reveals his health status; "individual" means a living individual; "information and communication network" – (a) means a network for the transmission of messages; and (b) includes a telecommunication network; "Minister" means the Minister to whom responsibility for the subject of data protection is assigned; "network" means a communication transmission system that provides interconnection among a number of local and remote devices; "Office" means the Data Protection Office referred to in section 4; "personal data" means any information relating to a data subject; "personal data breach" means a breach of security leading to the accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to, personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed; "proceedings" – (a) means any proceedings conducted by or under the supervision of a Judge or Magistrate; and (b) may include – (i) an inquiry or investigation into an offence; and (ii) disciplinary proceedings; "processor" means a person who, or public body which, processes personal data on behalf of a controller; "processing" means an operation or set of operations performed on personal data or sets of personal data, whether or not by automated means, such as collection, recording, organisation, structuring, storage, adaptation or alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure or destruction; "profiling" means any form of automated processing of personal data consisting of the use of personal data to evaluate certain personal aspects relating to an individual, in particular to analyse or predict aspects concerning that individual's performance at work, economic situation, health, personal preferences, interests, reliability, behaviour, location or movements; "pseudonymisation" means the processing of personal data in such a manner that the personal data can no longer be attributed to a specific data subject without the use of additional information and the additional information is kept separately and is subject to technical and organisational measures to ensure that the personal data are not attributed to an identified or identifiable individual; "recipient" means a person to whom, or a public body to which, personal data are disclosed, whether a third party or not; "register" means the register referred to in section 20; "registration certificate" means the registration certificate referred to in section 16 (2); "restriction of processing" means the marking of stored personal data with the aim of limiting their processing in the future; "special categories of personal data", in relation to a data subject, means personal data pertaining to – (a) his racial or ethnic origin; (b) his political opinion or adherence; (c) his religious or philosophical beliefs; (d) his membership of a trade union; (e) his physical or mental health or condition; (f) his sexual orientation, practices or preferences; (g) his genetic data or biometric data uniquely identifying him; (h) the commission or alleged commission of an offence by him; (i) any proceedings for an offence committed or alleged to have been committed by him, the disposal of such proceedings or the sentence of any Court in the proceedings; or (j) such other personal data as the Commissioner may determine to be sensitive personal data; "telecommunication network" means a system, or a series of systems, operating within such boundaries as may be prescribed, for the transmission or reception of messages by means of guided or unguided electro magnetic energy or both; "third party" means a person or public body other than a data subject, a controller, a processor or a person who, under the direct authority of a controller or processor, who or which is authorised to process personal data; "traffic data" means any data relating to a communication by means of a computer system and generated by the system that form part in the chain of communication, indicating the communication's origin, destination, route, time, date, size, duration, or type of underlying service; "Tribunal" means the ICT Appeal Tribunal set up under section 35 of the Information and Communication Technologies Act.

sections.heading: Application of Act
sections.title: Application of Act to Data Processing
sections.summary: This section defines when the Data Protection Act applies to personal data processing. It specifies that the Act binds the State, treats each government department as separate, applies to automated and non-automated processing where data forms part of a filing system, and excludes exchanges between government entities on a need-to-know basis and purely personal data processing activities. It also establishes criteria for when entities outside Mauritius are covered by the Act.

requirements.text: This Act shall bind the State
Each Ministry or Government department shall be treated as separate from any other Ministry or Government department

This Act shall apply to the processing of personal data, wholly or partly, by automated means and to any processing otherwise than by automated means where the personal data form part of a filing system or are intended to form part of a filing system
This Act shall not apply to the exchange of information between Ministries, Government departments and public sector agencies where such exchange is required on a need-to-know basis
This Act shall not apply to the processing of personal data by an individual in the course of a purely personal or household activity
This Act shall apply to a controller or processor who is established in Mauritius and processes personal data in the context of that establishment

This Act shall apply to a controller or processor who is not established in Mauritius but uses equipment in Mauritius for processing personal data, other than for the purpose of transit through Mauritius
Every controller or processor referred to in subsection (5)(b) shall nominate a representative established in Mauritius

For the purpose of subsection (5)(a), any person who is ordinarily resident in Mauritius or carries out data processing operations through an office, branch or agency in Mauritius shall be treated as being established in Mauritius

sections.source_quote: 3. Application of Act (1) This Act shall bind the State. (2) For the purposes of this Act, each Ministry or Government department shall be treated as separate from any other Ministry or Government department. (3) This Act shall apply to the processing of personal data, wholly or partly, by automated means and to any processing otherwise than by automated means where the personal data form part of a filing system or are intended to form part of a filing system. (4) This Act shall not apply to – (a) the exchange of information between Ministries, Government departments and public sector agencies where such exchange is required on a need-to-know basis; (b) the processing of personal data by an individual in the course of a purely personal or household activity. (5) Subject to section 44, this Act shall apply to a controller or processor who – (a) is established in Mauritius and processes personal data in the context of that establishment; and (b) is not established in Mauritius but uses equipment in Mauritius for processing personal data, other than for the purpose of transit through Mauritius.

sections.heading: Establishment of Office
sections.title: Establishment of Data Protection Office
sections.summary: This section establishes the Data Protection Office as a public office with complete independence and impartiality. It specifies that the head of the Office must be a barrister with at least five years' standing and that the Office shall be assisted by public officers under the Commissioner's administrative control. The Office is designed to ensure compliance with the Act, issue guidelines, maintain registers, and exercise oversight over data processing operations.

requirements.text: There shall, for the purposes of this Act, be a public office to be known as the Data Protection Office

In the discharge of its functions under this Act, the Office shall act with complete independence and impartiality and shall not be subject to the control or direction of any other person or authority
The head of the Office, who shall be known as the Data Protection Commissioner, shall be a barrister of not less than 5 years' standing
The Commissioner shall be assisted by such public officers as may be necessary
Every public officer referred to in subsection (4) shall be under the administrative control of the Commissioner

sections.source_quote: 4. Establishment of Office (1) There shall, for the purposes of this Act, be a public office to be known as the Data Protection Office. (2) In the discharge of its functions under this Act, the Office shall act with complete independence and impartiality and shall not be subject to the control or direction of any other person or authority. (3) The head of the Office, who shall be known as the Data Protection Commissioner, shall be a barrister of not less than 5 years' standing. (4) The Commissioner shall be assisted by such public officers as may be necessary. (5) Every public officer referred to in subsection (4) shall be under the administrative control of the Commissioner.

sections.heading: Functions of Commissioner
sections.title: Functions of Data Protection Commissioner
sections.summary: This section outlines the Commissioner's responsibilities including ensuring compliance with the Act, issuing guidelines, maintaining registers of controllers and processors, exercising control over data processing operations, promoting self-regulation, investigating complaints, taking measures to publicize the Act, monitoring data processing developments, and examining proposals for automated decision making or data linkage that could impact privacy.

requirements.text: The Commissioner shall ensure compliance with this Act and any regulations made under it
The Commissioner shall issue or approve such Codes of Practice or Guidelines for the purposes of this Act as he thinks fit
The Commissioner shall maintain a register of controllers and processors

The Commissioner shall exercise control on all data processing operations, either of his own motion or at the request of a data subject, and verify whether the processing of data is done in accordance with this Act
The Commissioner shall promote self-regulation among controllers and processors
The Commissioner shall investigate any complaint or information which gives rise to a suspicion that an offence may have been, is being or is about to be, committed under this Act
The Commissioner shall take such measures as may be necessary to bring the provisions of this Act to the knowledge of the general public

The Commissioner shall undertake research into, and monitor developments in, data processing, and ensure that there is no significant risk or adverse effect of any developments on the privacy of individuals

The Commissioner shall examine any proposal for automated decision making or data linkage that may involve an interference with, or may otherwise have an adverse effect, on the privacy of individuals and ensure that any adverse effect of the proposal on the privacy of individuals is minimised

The Commissioner shall cooperate with supervisory authorities of other countries, to the extent necessary for the performance of his duties under this Act, in particular by exchanging relevant information in accordance with any other enactment
The Commissioner shall do anything incidental or conducive to the attainment of the objects of and to the better performance of his duties and functions under this Act

sections.source_quote: 5. Functions of Commissioner The Commissioner shall – (a) ensure compliance with this Act and any regulations made under it; (b) issue or approve such Codes of Practice or Guidelines for the purposes of this Act as he thinks fit; (c) maintain a register of controllers and processors; (d) exercise control on all data processing operations, either of his own motion or at the request of a data subject, and verify whether the processing of data is done in accordance with this Act; (e) promote self-regulation among controllers and processors; (f) investigate any complaint or information which gives rise to a suspicion that an offence may have been, is being or is about to be, committed under this Act; (g) take such measures as may be necessary to bring the provisions of this Act to the knowledge of the general public; (h) undertake research into, and monitor developments in, data processing, and ensure that there is no significant risk or adverse effect of any developments on the privacy of individuals; (i) examine any proposal for automated decision making or data linkage that may involve an interference with, or may otherwise have an adverse effect, on the privacy of individuals and ensure that any adverse effect of the proposal on the privacy of individuals is minimised; (j) cooperate with supervisory authorities of other countries, to the extent necessary for the performance of his duties under this Act, in particular by exchanging relevant information in accordance with any other enactment; and (k) do anything incidental or conducive to the attainment of the objects of and to the better performance of his duties and functions under, this Act.

sections.heading: Investigation of complaints
sections.title: Investigation of complaints: complaint handling procedures
sections.summary: The Commissioner must investigate complaints about alleged contraventions of the Act or its regulations. If the complaint is not resolved amicably, the Commissioner must notify the complainant of their decision and allow an appeal under section 51. The Commissioner may order individuals to attend examinations, produce documents, or submit sworn statements. All orders must be written and signed. Failure to comply with orders is an offence punishable by fines up to 50,000 rupees and imprisonment for up to two years. The Commissioner may also take copies of documents and require access to digital information. No person may be compelled to answer questions that could incriminate them.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines procedures for investigating complaints and enforcing compliance, which may impact data transfer assessments by requiring access to data for investigations and imposing obligations on data controllers/processors during investigations.

requirements.text: The Commissioner shall investigate complaints or cause them to be investigated by an authorised officer unless the complaint is frivolous or vexatious
The Commissioner shall notify the complainant in writing of the decision if unable to resolve the complaint amicably within a reasonable time
The Commissioner may order a person to attend for examination
The Commissioner may order a person to produce documents relevant to the investigation
The Commissioner may order a person to submit a sworn or affirmed statement
All orders must be written and signed by the Commissioner or an authorised officer
A person served with an order must comply with it
A person served with an order must attend as directed
A person served with an order must answer questions and provide information as ordered
The Commissioner may take copies of documents produced under orders
The Commissioner may require digital information to be produced in a visible and legible form
Failure to attend, produce documents, or comply with orders without lawful excuse is an offence punishable by fines up to 50,000 rupees and imprisonment up to two years
No person may be required to answer questions that could incriminate them

sections.source_quote: Where a complaint is made to the Commissioner that this Act or any regulations made under it, has or have been, is or are being, or is or are about to be, contravened, the Commissioner shall – (a) investigate into the complaint or cause it to be investigated by an authorised officer, unless he is of the opinion that the complaint is frivolous or vexatious; and (b) where he is unable to arrange, within a reasonable time, for the amicable resolution by the parties concerned of the complaint, notify, in writing, the individual who made the complaint of his decision in relation to it so that the individual may, where he considers that he is aggrieved by the decision, appeal against it under section 51.

sections.heading: Power to require information
sections.title: Power to require information: data collection authority
sections.summary: The Commissioner may request information necessary for their functions from individuals or entities, including digital data. The requested information must be provided in a visible and legible form. Failure to comply or providing false information is an offence punishable by fines up to 50,000 rupees and imprisonment for up to two years. This power is subject to specific banking and financial regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows the Commissioner to collect data for investigations, which may impact data transfer assessments by requiring data access and imposing obligations on data controllers/processors during investigations.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may request information necessary for the discharge of functions and exercise of powers under this Act
The person named in the notice must produce or give access to digital information in a visible and legible form
Failure to comply with a request or providing false information is an offence punishable by fines up to 50,000 rupees and imprisonment up to two years

sections.source_quote: Subject to section 26 of the Bank of Mauritius Act, section 64 of the Banking Act, section 83 of the Financial Services Act, section 30 of the Financial Intelligence and Anti-Money Laundering Act and section 81 of the Prevention of Corruption Act – (a) the Commissioner may, by written notice served on a person, request from that person such information as is necessary or expedient for the discharge of his functions and the exercise of his powers under this Act; and (b) where the information requested by the Commissioner is stored in a computer, disc or cassette, or on microfilm, or preserved by any mechanical or electronic device, the person named in the notice shall produce or give access to the information in a form in which it can be taken away and in which it is visible and legible.

sections.heading: Preservation Order
sections.title: Preservation Order: data preservation for investigations
sections.summary: The Commissioner may apply to a Judge for a Preservation Order to preserve data vulnerable to loss or modification. The order lasts up to 90 days and may be extended. This section applies to data including traffic data and is part of the Commissioner's powers to ensure data integrity during investigations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables data preservation during investigations, which may impact data transfer assessments by requiring data to be preserved for specific periods and potentially affecting data storage and retention policies.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may apply to a Judge for a Preservation Order for data vulnerable to loss or modification
The Preservation Order specifies a period not exceeding 90 days
The Judge may extend the Preservation Order for a period as deemed fit

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner may apply to a Judge in Chambers for a Preservation Order for the expeditious preservation of data, including traffic data, where he has reasonable ground to believe that the data are vulnerable to loss or modification.

sections.heading: Enforcement notice
sections.title: Enforcement notice: corrective actions for violations
sections.summary: The Commissioner may serve an enforcement notice requiring data controllers or processors to take corrective actions within 21 days to address alleged violations. The notice specifies the violated provision, required remedial steps, and a 21-day compliance period. Non-compliance is an offence punishable by fines up to 50,000 rupees and imprisonment for up to two years. The Commissioner may vary the notice if compliance is not feasible.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates corrective actions for violations, which may impact data transfer assessments by requiring data controllers/processors to implement specific measures and potentially affecting data storage and processing.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may serve an enforcement notice requiring corrective actions within a specified period
The enforcement notice must specify the violated provision, required remedial steps, and a 21-day compliance period
Non-compliance with an enforcement notice is an offence punishable by fines up to 50,000 rupees and imprisonment up to two years
The Commissioner may vary the enforcement notice if compliance is not feasible

sections.source_quote: Where the Commissioner is of the opinion that a controller or a processor has contravened, is contravening or is about to contravene this Act, the Commissioner may serve an enforcement notice on him requiring him to take such steps within such period as may be specified in the notice.

sections.heading: Power to seek assistance
sections.title: Power to seek assistance: investigation support
sections.summary: The Commissioner may seek assistance from persons or authorities for investigations, including gathering information. Those assisting are considered authorised officers under section 49. This power supports the Commissioner's ability to conduct investigations and enforce compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables the Commissioner to gather information during investigations, which may impact data transfer assessments by requiring data access and potentially affecting data collection and processing.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may seek assistance from persons or authorities for investigations
Persons assisting the Commissioner are considered authorised officers under section 49

sections.source_quote: For the purpose of gathering information or for the proper conduct of any investigation under this Act, the Commissioner may seek the assistance of such person or authority as he thinks fit and that person or authority may do such things as are reasonably necessary to assist the Commissioner in the discharge of his functions.

sections.heading: Power of entry and search
sections.title: Power of entry and search: physical data access
sections.summary: Authorised officers may enter and search premises for data collection purposes with a warrant from a Magistrate. They may request documents, examine data, and take copies. Digital data must be produced in a visible and legible form. This section enables physical data access during investigations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows physical data access during investigations, which may impact data transfer assessments by requiring data to be accessible for inspection and potentially affecting data storage and processing.

requirements.text: An authorised officer may enter and search premises for data collection purposes
An authorised officer must show a warrant issued by a Magistrate to enter or search premises
A Magistrate may issue a warrant authorising entry and search if necessary for the Commissioner's functions
A warrant issued under subsection (3) is valid for the period stated and may have conditions
An authorised officer may request documents, examine data, and take copies
Digital data must be produced in a visible and legible form

sections.source_quote: Subject to this section, an authorised officer may enter and search any premises for the purpose of discharging any function or exercising any power under this Act.

sections.heading: Obstruction of Commissioner or authorised officer
sections.title: Obstruction of Commissioner or authorized officer
sections.summary: Any person obstructing the Commissioner or authorized officer in exercising powers under section 11, failing to provide requested assistance or information, refusing entry to premises, or providing false/misleading information commits an offence. Offenders face fines up to 50,000 rupees and imprisonment up to 2 years.

requirements.text: Obstructs or impedes the Commissioner or authorized officer in exercising powers under section 11
Fails to provide assistance or information requested by Commissioner or authorized officer
Refuses to allow Commissioner or authorized officer to enter premises or take persons
Gives false or misleading information in a material particular to Commissioner or authorized officer

sections.source_quote: Any person who, in relation to the exercise of a power conferred by section 11 – (a) obstructs or impedes the Commissioner or an authorised officer in the exercise of such power; (b) fails to provide assistance or information requested by the Commissioner or authorised officer; (c) refuses to allow the Commissioner or an authorised officer to enter any premises or to take any person with him in the exercise of his functions; (d) gives to the Commissioner or an authorised officer any information which is false or misleading in a material particular

sections.heading: Delegation of power by Commissioner
sections.title: Delegation of power by Commissioner
sections.summary: The Commissioner may delegate investigating or enforcement powers to an officer of the Office or a police officer designated by the Commissioner of Police for specific purposes.

requirements.text: Commissioner may delegate investigating or enforcement powers to an officer of the Office
Commissioner may delegate investigating or enforcement powers to a police officer designated by Commissioner of Police

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner may delegate any investigating or enforcement power conferred on him by this Act to an officer of the Office or to a police officer designated for that purpose by the Commissioner of Police.

sections.heading: Application for registration
sections.title: Application for registration
sections.summary: Controllers and processors must apply to the Commissioner with specific details including personal data description, data subjects, special categories, processing purposes, data recipients, transfer countries, and risk/safeguards. Knowingly providing false information commits an offence with fines up to 100,000 rupees and imprisonment up to 5 years.

requirements.text: Apply to Commissioner in approved form to be registered as controller or processor
Provide name and address
Provide representative name and address if nominated
Describe personal data to be processed and data subjects
State whether special categories of personal data are held or likely to be held
Describe purpose for processing personal data
Describe any recipients to whom data may be disclosed
State any country to which data may be transferred directly or indirectly
Provide general description of risks, safeguards, security measures
Knowingly supplying false or misleading information commits an offence

sections.source_quote: Every person who intends to act as a controller or processor shall apply to the Commissioner, in such form as the Commissioner may approve, to be registered as controller or processor.

sections.heading: Issue of registration certificate
sections.title: Issue of registration certificate
sections.summary: The Commissioner grants registration if criteria are met and issues a certificate valid for 3 years upon payment of fees. Registration requires compliance with specified terms and conditions.

requirements.text: Commissioner grants application for registration as controller or processor
Issue registration certificate on payment of prescribed fee
Registration certificate valid for 3 years

sections.source_quote: Where the Commissioner considers that an applicant meets the criteria to be registered as a controller or processor, as the case may be, he shall grant the application.

sections.heading: Change in particulars
sections.title: Change in particulars
sections.summary: Controllers and processors must notify the Commissioner within 14 days of any changes to application details. The Commissioner amends the register upon verification of changes. Failure to notify commits an offence with fines up to 50,000 rupees.

requirements.text: Notify Commissioner in writing within 14 days of any change in particulars
Commissioner amends register upon satisfaction of change in particulars
Failure to comply with notification commits an offence

sections.source_quote: Where, following the grant of an application, there is a change in any of the particulars referred to in section 15(2), the controller or processor, shall, within 14 days of the date of the change, notify the Commissioner in writing of the nature and date of the change.

sections.heading: Renewal of registration certificate
sections.title: Renewal of registration certificate
sections.summary: Registrants may apply for renewal up to 3 months before certificate expiry. The Commissioner issues a new certificate upon payment of fees and meeting specified terms and conditions.

requirements.text: Apply for renewal of certificate not later than 3 months before expiry
Issue new registration certificate on payment of prescribed fee

sections.source_quote: The holder of a registration certificate may apply for the renewal of the certificate not later than 3 months before the date of its expiry.

sections.heading: Cancellation or variation of terms and conditions of
sections.title: Cancellation of registration certificates for false information or non-compliance
sections.summary: The Commissioner may cancel or vary a registration certificate if the applicant provided false information or the holder failed to comply with the Act or certificate terms without lawful excuse. The holder must show cause within 14 days before cancellation or variation occurs.
sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for DTIA as it outlines conditions under which registration certificates can be revoked, impacting data processing authority and requiring due diligence on data subject compliance.

requirements.text: Commissioner may cancel registration certificate or vary terms if applicant provided false information
Commissioner may cancel registration certificate or vary terms if holder fails to comply with Act or certificate terms without lawful excuse
Commissioner must require written show cause within 14 days before cancellation or variation

sections.source_quote: Subject to this section, the Commissioner may cancel a registration certificate or vary its terms and conditions where – (a) any information given to him by the applicant is false or misleading in any material particular; (b) the holder of the registration certificate fails, without lawful excuse, to comply with – (i) any requirement of this Act; or (ii) any term or condition specified in the certificate. The Commissioner shall, before cancelling or varying the terms and conditions of a registration certificate, require, by notice in writing, the holder of the certificate to show cause, within 14 days of the notice, why the registration certificate should not be cancelled or its terms and conditions should not be varied

sections.heading: Register of controllers and processors
sections.title: Public register for data protection controllers and processors
sections.summary: A public Data Protection Register must be maintained by the Commissioner, allowing free inspection and certified copies for fee payment. The Commissioner may remove details of ceased entities from the register upon request.
sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for DTIA as it establishes transparency requirements for data processing entities, impacting data subject visibility and regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: Commissioner must maintain a public Data Protection Register
Commissioner may remove details of ceased entities from register upon request
Register must be available for free inspection by any person
Any person may obtain certified copy of register entry for fee payment

sections.source_quote: There shall be a register of controllers and processors to be known as the Data Protection Register, which shall be kept and maintained by the Commissioner in such form and manner as he may determine. The Commissioner may, at any time, at the request of a controller or processor, in respect of which there is an entry in the register and which has ceased to exist, remove its details from the register. The register shall, at all reasonable times, be available for inspection by any person free of charge. Any person may, on payment of such fee as may be prescribed, obtain from the Commissioner a certified copy of, or of an extract from, any entry in the register.

sections.heading: Principles relating to processing of personal data
sections.title: Principles for lawful processing of personal data
sections.summary: Controllers must ensure personal data processing complies with six principles: lawfulness and transparency, explicit legitimate purposes, relevance and minimization, accuracy and timeliness, limited retention, and respect for data subject rights.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical for DTIA as it defines core data processing principles that must be adhered to across all data handling activities, including cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: Personal data must be processed lawfully, fairly and transparently
Personal data must be collected for explicit, specified and legitimate purposes
Personal data must be adequate, relevant and limited to necessary purposes
Personal data must be accurate and kept up to date with timely corrections
Personal data must be kept for no longer than necessary for processing purposes
Personal data must be processed in accordance with data subject rights

sections.source_quote: Every controller or processor shall ensure that personal data are – (a) processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to any data subject; (b) collected for explicit, specified and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner incompatible with those purposes; (c) adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed; (d) accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date, with every reasonable step being taken to ensure that any inaccurate personal data are erased or rectified without delay; (e) kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; and (f) processed in accordance with the rights of data subjects.

sections.heading: Duties of controller
sections.title: Controller's data protection duties and measures
sections.summary: Controllers must implement technical and organizational measures to ensure compliance with the Act, including data security, record-keeping, data protection impact assessments, prior authorization compliance, and designating a data protection officer.
sections.dtia_summary: Directly relevant for DTIA as it mandates specific technical and organizational measures for data processing, including impact assessments and compliance verification.

requirements.text: Controllers must adopt policies and implement technical/organizational measures for compliance
Technical measures must align with section 31 (data security)
Controllers must keep records of all processing operations per section 33
Controllers must perform data protection impact assessments per section 34
Controllers must comply with prior authorization requirements per section 35
Controllers must designate an officer responsible for data protection compliance

sections.source_quote: Every controller shall adopt policies and implement appropriate technical and organisational measures so as to ensure and be able to demonstrate that the processing of personal data is performed in accordance with this Act. The measures referred to in subsection (1) shall include – (a) implementing appropriate data security and organisational measures in accordance with section 31; (b) keeping a record of all processing operations in accordance with section 33; (c) performing a data protection impact assessment in accordance with section 34; (d) complying with the requirements for prior authorisation from, or consultation with the Commissioner pursuant to section 35; and (e) designating an officer responsible for data protection compliance issues.

sections.heading: Collection of personal data
sections.title: Rules for collecting personal data from data subjects
sections.summary: Controllers may not collect personal data unless it serves a lawful purpose and is necessary for that purpose. Direct collection requires informing data subjects of specific details including purpose, recipients, consent status, rights, and data retention periods, with exceptions for pre-existing information or legal requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical for DTIA as it details requirements for data collection, including transparency and consent mechanisms that impact cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Controller may not collect personal data unless it serves a lawful purpose connected with controller's function
Controller may not collect personal data unless it is necessary for that purpose

Controller must inform data subject of identity, contact details, purpose, recipients, consent status, withdrawal rights, data subject rights, automated decisions, retention period, complaint rights, and cross-border transfers
Controller must not be required to comply with subsection (2) if data subject already has the information
Controller must not be required to comply with subsection (2) if data collection is impossible or disproportionate effort
Controller must not be required to comply with subsection (2) if data disclosure is legally mandated

sections.source_quote: Subject to section 44, a controller shall not collect personal data unless – (a) it is done for a lawful purpose connected with a function or activity of the controller; and (b) the collection of the data is necessary for that purpose. Subject to subsection (3), where a controller collects personal data directly from a data subject, the controller shall, at the time of collecting the personal data, ensure that the data subject concerned is informed of – (a) the identity and contact details of the controller and, where applicable, its representative and any data protection officer; (b) the purpose for which the data are being collected; (c) the intended recipients of the data; (d) whether or not the supply of the data by that data subject is voluntary or mandatory; (e) the existence of the right to withdraw consent at any time, without affecting the lawfulness of processing based on consent before its withdrawal; (f) the existence of the right to request from the controller access to and rectification, restriction or erasure of personal data concerning the data subject or to object to the processing; (g) the existence of automated decision making, including profiling, and information about the logic involved, as well as the significance and the envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject; (h) the period for which the personal data shall be stored; (i) the right to lodge a complaint with the Commissioner; (j) where applicable, that the controller intends to transfer personal data to another country and on the level of suitable protection afforded by that country; and (k) any further information necessary to guarantee fair processing in respect of the data subject's personal data, having regard to the specific circumstances in which the data are collected.

sections.heading: Conditions for consent
sections.title: Conditions for valid consent in data processing
sections.summary: Controllers bear the burden of proof for data subject consent, which may be withdrawn at any time. Consent must be freely given, with special consideration for cases where contract performance is conditional on consent.
sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for DTIA as it defines consent requirements that impact data processing legitimacy and cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: Controller bears burden of proof for data subject consent
Data subject may withdraw consent at any time
Consent must be freely given, with consideration for contractual conditions

sections.source_quote: The controller shall bear the burden of proof for establishing a data subject's consent to the processing of his personal data for a specified purpose. The data subject shall have the right to withdraw his consent at any time. In determining whether consent was freely given, account shall be taken of whether, inter alia, the performance of a contract, including the provision of a service, is conditional on consent to the processing of personal data that is not necessary for the performance of that contract.

sections.heading: Notification of personal data breach
sections.title: Notification of personal data breach to Commissioner
sections.summary: Controllers must notify the Commissioner within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach, including details on affected data subjects and records. Processors must notify controllers without undue delay. Notifications must describe the breach, provide contact details for the data protection officer, and recommend remedial actions. Controllers must specify facts to enable verification of compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely breach notification to the Commissioner with specific details, impacting DTIA by mandating breach reporting timelines and content for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Controller must notify Commissioner within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach
Controller must provide reasons for delay if notification is late
Processor must notify controller without undue delay upon becoming aware of breach
Notification must describe nature of breach, affected data subjects and records
Notification must include contact details of data protection officer
Notification must recommend measures to address breach and mitigate adverse effects
Controller must specify facts to enable Commissioner verification of compliance

sections.source_quote: 25. Notification of personal data breach (1) (a) In the case of a personal data breach, the controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the personal data breach to the Commissioner.

sections.heading: Communication of personal data breach to data subject
sections.title: Communication of personal data breach to data subject
sections.summary: Controllers must inform data subjects of a breach likely to cause high risk to their rights if they have not already done so, after notifying the Commissioner. Communications must be in clear language and include details from section 25. Exceptions include when technical measures like encryption prevent breach impact, subsequent measures mitigate risk, or public communication is equally effective.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines when and how data subjects must be notified of breaches, impacting DTIA by requiring breach communication protocols for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Controller must communicate breach to data subject without undue delay if breach likely to cause high risk to rights
Communication must describe breach in clear language and include information from section 25
No communication required if controller has implemented encryption or other technical measures
No communication required if subsequent measures mitigate high risk
No communication required if disproportionate effort and public communication is equally effective
Commissioner may require communication if controller has not already done so

sections.source_quote: 26. Communication of personal data breach to data subject (1) Subject to subsection (3), where a personal data breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of a data subject, the controller shall, after the notification referred to in section 25, communicate the personal data breach to the data subject without undue delay.

sections.heading: Duty to destroy personal data
sections.title: Duty to destroy personal data
sections.summary: Controllers must destroy personal data as soon as reasonably practicable when the purpose for keeping it lapses, and notify processors holding the data. Processors must destroy specified data as soon as reasonably practicable upon receiving such notification.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires data destruction upon purpose lapse, impacting DTIA by mandating deletion timelines and notification for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Controller must destroy personal data as soon as reasonably practicable when purpose lapses
Controller must notify processors holding the data
Processor must destroy specified data as soon as reasonably practicable

sections.source_quote: 27. Duty to destroy personal data (1) Where the purpose for keeping personal data has lapsed, every controller shall – (a) destroy the data as soon as is reasonably practicable; and (b) notify any processor holding the data.

sections.heading: Lawful processing
sections.title: Lawful processing of personal data
sections.summary: Personal data may only be processed if consent is given, processing is necessary for a contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public interest, legitimate interests, or historical/statistical research. Violations incur fines up to 100,000 rupees and imprisonment up to 5 years.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines lawful processing conditions, impacting DTIA by requiring consent or legal basis for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Personal data may only be processed with consent from data subject
Processing must be necessary for performance of contract
Processing must be necessary for compliance with legal obligation
Processing must be necessary to protect vital interests of data subject or another person
Processing must be necessary for public interest or official authority
Processing must be necessary for legitimate interests of controller or third party
Processing must be necessary for historical, statistical or scientific research

sections.source_quote: 28. Lawful processing (1) No person shall process personal data unless – (a) the data subject consents to the processing for one or more specified purposes; (b) the processing is necessary – (i) for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is a party or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject before entering into a contract; (ii) for compliance with any legal obligation to which the controller is subject; (iii) in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or another person; (iv) for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller; (v) the performance of any task carried out by a public authority; (vi) the exercise, by any person in the public interest, of any other functions of a public nature; (vii) for the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party to whom the data are disclosed, except if the processing is unwarranted in any particular case having regard to the harm and prejudice to the rights and freedoms or legitimate interests of the data subject; or (vii) for the purpose of historical, statistical or scientific research.

sections.heading: Special categories of personal data
sections.title: Special categories of personal data processing
sections.summary: Special categories of personal data may only be processed if consent is given, processing is necessary for legal claims, health care, or specific rights, or the data subject is incapable of consent. Processing must be conducted by not-for-profit bodies with safeguards and limited disclosure.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts processing of special categories, impacting DTIA by requiring specific safeguards for sensitive data transfers.

requirements.text: Special categories may only be processed if section 28 applies
Processing must be carried out by not-for-profit bodies with political, philosophical, religious or trade union aims
Processing must relate solely to members or former members of the body
Processing must not disclose data outside the body without consent
Processing may be necessary for legal claims
Processing may be necessary for health care, medical diagnosis, treatment or social care

sections.source_quote: 29.
