Special categories of personal data (1) Special categories of personal data shall not be processed unless – (a) section 28 applies to the processing; and (b) the processing is carried out in the course of its legitimate activities with appropriate safeguards by a foundation, association or any other not-for-profit body with a political, philosophical, religious or trade union aim and on condition that the processing relates solely to the members or to former members of the body or to persons who have regular contact with it in connection with its purposes and that the personal data are not disclosed outside that body without the consent of the data subjects; (c) the processing relates to personal data which are manifestly made public by the data subject; or (d) the processing is necessary for – (i) the establishment, exercise or defence of a legal claim; (ii) the purpose of preventive or occupational medicine, for the assessment of the working capacity of an employee, medical diagnosis, the provision of health or social care or treatment or the management of health or social care systems and services or pursuant to a contract with a health professional and subject to the conditions and safeguards referred to in subsection (2); (iii) the purpose of carrying out the obligations and exercising specific rights of the controller or of the data subject; or (iv) protecting the vital interests of the data subject or of another person where the data subject is physically or legally incapable of giving consent.

sections.heading: Personal data of child
sections.title: Personal data of child below 16 years
sections.summary: Personal data of children below 16 years may only be processed with consent from parents or guardians. Controllers must verify consent through reasonable efforts considering available technology.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires parental consent for children under 16, impacting DTIA by mandating consent verification for cross-border data transfers involving minors.

requirements.text: Personal data of children below 16 years may only be processed with consent from parent or guardian
Controller must verify consent through reasonable efforts considering available technology

sections.source_quote: 30. Personal data of child (1) No person shall process the personal data of a child below the age of 16 years unless consent is given by the child's parent or guardian.

sections.heading: Security of processing
sections.title: Security measures for data processing
sections.summary: Controllers and processors must implement security and organisational measures to prevent unauthorised access, alteration, disclosure, accidental loss, or destruction of data they control. These measures must be appropriate for the potential harm and the nature of the data. The measures include pseudonymisation, encryption, system resilience, and regular testing of security protocols. Controllers must also ensure processors provide sufficient security guarantees and enter written contracts with specific obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires robust security measures for data processing, including encryption and regular testing, which are critical for DTIAs involving cross-border data transfers to ensure adequate protection.

requirements.text: Controllers and processors must implement security and organisational measures to prevent unauthorised access to data
Controllers and processors must implement security and organisational measures to prevent alteration of data
Controllers and processors must implement security and organisational measures to prevent disclosure of data
Controllers and processors must implement security and organisational measures to prevent accidental loss of data
Controllers and processors must implement security and organisational measures to prevent destruction of data
Security measures must provide a level of security appropriate for the harm that might result from unauthorised access to data
Security measures must provide a level of security appropriate for the harm that might result from alteration of data
Security measures must provide a level of security appropriate for the harm that might result from disclosure of data
Security measures must provide a level of security appropriate for the harm that might result from destruction of data
Security measures must provide a level of security appropriate for the nature of the data concerned
Security measures must include pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data
Security measures must include the ability to ensure ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems
Security measures must include the ability to restore availability and access to personal data in a timely manner after incidents
Security measures must include a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating technical and organisational measures
The Office may lay down technical standards for security measures
Controllers must consider technological development, cost, special risks, and data nature when determining security measures
Controllers must choose processors providing sufficient security guarantees
Controllers and processors must enter written contracts specifying processor actions only on controller instructions
Controllers and processors must enter written contracts specifying processor obligations under section 31
Processors must be considered controllers if they process data without controller instructions
Controllers and processors must ensure employees are aware and comply with security measures

sections.source_quote: A controller or processor shall, at the time of the determination of the means for processing and at the time of the processing – implement appropriate security and organisational measures for – (i) the prevention of unauthorised access to; (ii) the alteration of; (iii) the disclosure of; (iv) the accidental loss of; and (v) the destruction of, the data in his control

sections.heading: Prior security check
sections.title: Prior security inspection by regulator
sections.summary: The Commissioner may inspect and assess security measures under section 31 before processing or data transfers begin if they pose specific risks to data subjects' privacy rights. The Commissioner can also conduct further inspections during working hours to ensure ongoing compliance with security measures.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows regulator to verify security measures before data transfers, ensuring DTIAs meet necessary safeguards for cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: Commissioner may inspect and assess security measures under section 31 before processing or data transfers begin
Commissioner may conduct further inspections during working hours to ensure ongoing compliance

sections.source_quote: Where the Commissioner is of the opinion that the processing or transfer of data by a controller or processor may entail a specific risk to the privacy rights of data subjects, he may inspect and assess the security measures taken under section 31 prior to the beginning of the processing or transfer.

sections.heading: Record of processing operations
sections.title: Record of processing operations
sections.summary: Controllers and processors must maintain detailed records of all processing activities, including controller details, processing purposes, data categories, recipients, data transfers to other countries, erasure timelines, and mechanisms from section 22. These records must be available to the Office upon request.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires comprehensive records for DTIAs to track data flows, transfers, and safeguards, ensuring transparency and accountability in cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Controllers and processors must maintain a record of all processing operations under their responsibility
Records must include controller/processor name and contact details, and data protection officer information
Records must describe the purpose of processing
Records must describe categories of data subjects and personal data
Records must describe categories of recipients, including international recipients
Records must include any transfers of data to another country and suitable safeguards for transfers under section 36
Records must specify envisaged time limits for erasure of different data categories
Records must describe mechanisms from section 22(3)
Controllers and processors must make records available to the Office upon request

sections.source_quote: Every controller or processor shall maintain a record of all processing operations under his or its responsibility.

sections.heading: Data protection impact assessment
sections.title: Data protection impact assessment for high-risk processing
sections.summary: Controllers and processors must conduct data protection impact assessments before processing operations that pose high risks to data subjects' rights and freedoms, such as large-scale profiling, special category data processing, or systematic monitoring. The assessment must evaluate necessity, proportionality, risks, and risk-mitigation measures, including safeguards and security protocols.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates DTIAs for high-risk processing, ensuring adequate safeguards for cross-border data transfers and sensitive data handling.

requirements.text: Controllers and processors must conduct data protection impact assessments before high-risk processing operations
High-risk processing includes systematic and extensive automated profiling with legal effects
High-risk processing includes large-scale processing of special categories of data
High-risk processing includes systematic large-scale monitoring of publicly accessible areas
High-risk processing includes other operations requiring consultation with the Office
Assessments must describe envisaged processing operations and purposes
Assessments must evaluate necessity and proportionality of processing operations
Assessments must evaluate risks to data subjects' rights and freedoms
Assessments must include measures to address risks and safeguards for data protection

sections.source_quote: Where processing operations are likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of data subjects by virtue of their nature, scope, context and purposes, every controller or processor shall, prior to the processing, carry out an assessment of the impact of the envisaged processing operations on the protection of personal data.

sections.heading: Prior authorisation and consultation
sections.title: Prior authorisation and consultation for data processing
sections.summary: Controllers and processors must obtain prior authorisation from the Commissioner before processing personal data, especially when transfers to other countries are involved. They must also consult the Commissioner if data protection impact assessments indicate high risks or if the Commissioner deems consultation necessary. The Commissioner can prohibit non-compliant processing and require remedial actions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires prior authorisation for cross-border data transfers, ensuring DTIAs meet regulatory standards and mitigate risks for international data flows.

requirements.text: Controllers and processors must obtain authorisation from the Commissioner before processing personal data
Controllers and processors must consult the Commissioner before processing personal data if data protection impact assessments indicate high risks
Controllers and processors must consult the Commissioner before processing personal data if the Commissioner deems consultation necessary
The Commissioner may prohibit processing if it does not comply with the Act
The Commissioner may require remedial actions for non-compliant processing

sections.source_quote: Every controller or processor shall obtain authorisation from the Office prior to processing personal data in order to ensure compliance of the intended processing with this Act and in particular to mitigate the risks involved for the data subjects where a controller or processor cannot provide for the appropriate safeguards referred to in section 36 in relation to the transfer of personal data to another country.

sections.heading: Transfer of personal data outside Mauritius
sections.title: Transfer of personal data outside Mauritius
sections.summary: Personal data may be transferred outside Mauritius if the Commissioner is satisfied with appropriate safeguards, data subjects provide explicit consent after being informed of risks, transfers are necessary for contractual or legal purposes, or transfers occur from public registers. The Commissioner may prohibit or condition transfers to protect data subjects' rights.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines conditions for cross-border data transfers, requiring safeguards, consent, or legal necessity, which are critical for DTIAs involving international data flows.

requirements.text: Transfers may occur if the Commissioner is satisfied with appropriate safeguards for data protection
Transfers may occur if data subjects provide explicit consent after being informed of transfer risks
Transfers may occur if necessary for contractual performance or pre-contractual measures
Transfers may occur if necessary for legal claims or public interest
Transfers may occur if necessary to protect vital interests of data subjects
Transfers may occur if legitimate interests are not overridden and safeguards are proven
Transfers from public registers must not involve entire data sets
The Commissioner may request proof of safeguards or compelling legitimate interests
The Commissioner may prohibit, suspend or condition transfers to protect data subjects' rights

sections.source_quote: A controller or processor may transfer personal data to another country where – (a) he or it has provided to the Commissioner proof of appropriate safeguards with respect to the protection of the personal data;

sections.heading: Right of access
sections.title: Data subject access to personal data records
sections.summary: Controllers must provide data subjects with free copies of their personal data upon written request, within one month, with possible extension for complex cases. They must also disclose processing purposes, data categories, recipients, retention periods, and rights to rectification or erasure. Controllers must use plain language and may charge fees for manifestly excessive requests, but must justify such fees.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires controllers to provide data subjects with access to their personal data and related processing information, impacting cross-border data transfers through transparency requirements for international data flows.

requirements.text: Controllers must provide data subjects with free copies of personal data upon written request within one month
Controllers must disclose processing purposes, data categories, recipients, retention periods, and rights to rectification or erasure
Controllers must provide information in plain language
Controllers may charge fees for manifestly excessive requests
Controllers must justify fees for manifestly excessive requests

sections.source_quote: Every controller shall, on the written request of a data subject provide, at reasonable intervals, without excessive delay and, subject to subsection (7), free of charge, confirmation as to whether or not personal data relating to the data subject are being processed and forward to him a copy of the data.

sections.heading: Automated individual decision making
sections.title: Prohibition on automated decisions affecting individuals
sections.summary: Data subjects have the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing that produce legal effects or significantly affect them. Exceptions include contractual necessity, legally authorized processing with safeguards, and explicit consent. Controllers must implement safeguards for automated processing involving special categories of data.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts automated decision-making that could impact individuals, requiring safeguards for cross-border data transfers involving profiling and automated processing.

requirements.text: Data subjects have the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing that produce legal effects or significantly affect them
Automated decisions for contractual purposes are exempt
Automated decisions based on legally authorized processing with safeguards are exempt
Automated decisions based on explicit consent are exempt
Automated processing of special categories of data is prohibited

sections.source_quote: Every data subject shall have the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produces legal effects concerning him or significantly affects him.

sections.heading: Rectification, erasure or restriction of processing
sections.title: Data rectification, erasure, and processing restrictions
sections.summary: Controllers must rectify inaccurate personal data, erase data when no longer necessary or upon data subject withdrawal of consent, and restrict processing under specific conditions. Controllers must inform third parties when erasing public data links and implement mechanisms to ensure timely compliance with data retention periods.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires controllers to manage data subject rights for erasure and restriction, impacting cross-border data transfers through data minimization and retention controls.

requirements.text: Controllers must rectify inaccurate personal data without undue delay
Controllers must erase data when no longer necessary for the purpose of processing
Controllers must erase data upon data subject withdrawal of consent
Controllers must erase data when data subject objects to processing and there are no overriding legitimate grounds
Controllers must erase data that has been unlawfully processed
Controllers must inform third parties when erasing public data links
Controllers must implement mechanisms to ensure timely compliance with data retention periods

sections.source_quote: A controller shall erase personal data without undue delay where the data are no longer necessary in relation to the purpose for which they were collected or otherwise processed.

sections.heading: Right to object
sections.title: Right to object to data processing
sections.summary: Data subjects can object to processing of personal data unless the controller demonstrates compelling legitimate grounds overriding their interests or for legal claims. Processing for direct marketing must cease upon objection, and these rights must be explicitly communicated to data subjects.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows data subjects to object to data processing, impacting cross-border data transfers through data minimization and purpose limitation.

requirements.text: Data subjects have the right to object to processing unless compelling legitimate grounds override their interests
Processing for direct marketing must cease upon objection
Controllers must explicitly communicate these rights to data subjects

sections.source_quote: The data subject shall have the right to object in writing at any time to the processing of personal data concerning him unless the controller demonstrates compelling legitimate grounds for the processing which override the data subject's interests, rights and freedoms or for the establishment, exercise or defence of a legal claim.

sections.heading: Exercise of rights
sections.title: Exercise of data subject rights by authorized persons
sections.summary: Data subjects may exercise their rights through authorized persons, including guardians for minors, legally appointed administrators for physically or mentally unfit individuals, or written authorizations from the data subject.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows data subjects to delegate rights to authorized persons, impacting cross-border data transfers through data subject representation.

requirements.text: Data subjects may exercise rights through a person with parental authority for minors
Data subjects may exercise rights through a legally appointed guardian for physically or mentally unfit individuals
Data subjects may exercise rights through a person duly authorized in writing

sections.source_quote: Any right conferred on an individual in this Act may be exercised where the data subject is a minor, by a person who has parental authority over the minor or has been appointed as his guardian

sections.heading: Unlawful disclosure of personal data
sections.title: Unlawful disclosure of personal data
sections.summary: Controllers and processors who disclose personal data without lawful excuse commit offences. Unauthorized access to personal data and disclosure to others also constitute offences, except for employees acting within their mandate. Offering to sell personal data obtained unlawfully is also prohibited.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibits unlawful disclosure of personal data, impacting cross-border data transfers through data security and confidentiality requirements.

requirements.text: Controllers who disclose personal data without lawful excuse commit offences
Processors who disclose personal data without controller's prior authority commit offences
Unauthorized access to personal data and disclosure to others commit offences
Employees acting within mandate are exempt from offences
Offering to sell personal data obtained unlawfully commits offences

sections.source_quote: Any controller who, without lawful excuse, discloses personal data in any manner that is incompatible with the purpose for which such data has been collected shall commit an offence.

sections.heading: Offence for which no specific penalty provided
sections.title: Penalties for contraventions without specific penalties
sections.summary: This section establishes penalties for individuals or entities that commit offences under the Act without specific penalties being outlined. Conviction may result in a fine up to 200,000 rupees and imprisonment for up to five years. Additionally, courts can order the forfeiture of equipment used in the offence or prohibit actions to stop ongoing contraventions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section sets general penalties for non-compliance, which could impact DTIA by imposing financial and operational consequences for breaches of data protection obligations.

requirements.text: Any person who commits an offence under this Act for which no specific penalty is provided or who otherwise contravenes this Act shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding 200,000 rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 5 years.
The Court may order the forfeiture of any equipment or any article used or connected in any way with the commission of an offence.
The Court may order or prohibit the doing of any act to stop a continuing contravention.

sections.source_quote: Any person who commits an offence under this Act for which no specific penalty is provided or who otherwise contravenes this Act shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding 200, 000 rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 5 years.

sections.heading: Exceptions and restrictions
sections.title: Exceptions and restrictions for data processing
sections.summary: This section outlines exceptions where data processing may be exempt from the Act's provisions. These include national security, public safety, criminal investigations, public interest, judicial independence, data subject rights, and specific COVID-19-related licenses. It also permits research activities with adequate security measures and allows data subjects or the Commissioner to seek judicial protection for rights.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines critical exceptions that could affect DTIA by allowing certain data transfers or processing activities without full compliance, particularly in national security or public interest contexts.

requirements.text: No exception to this Act shall be allowed except where it constitutes a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society for the protection of national security, defence or public security.

No exception to this Act shall be allowed except where it constitutes a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society for the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of an offence, including the execution of a penalty.

No exception to this Act shall be allowed except where it constitutes a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society for an objective of general public interest, including an economic or financial interest of the State.

No exception to this Act shall be allowed except where it constitutes a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society for the protection of judicial independence and judicial proceedings.

No exception to this Act shall be allowed except where it constitutes a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society for the protection of a data subject or the rights and freedoms of others.

No exception to this Act shall be allowed except where it constitutes a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society for the issue of any licence, permit or authorisation during the COVID-19 period.

The processing of personal data for the purpose of historical, statistical or scientific research may be exempt from the provisions of this Act where the security and organisational measures specified in section 31 are implemented to protect the rights and freedoms of data subjects involved.
Where this section has been breached, a data subject or the Commissioner may apply for a Judge's order to protect the rights of individuals.

Personal data shall be exempt from any provision of this Act where the non-application of such provision would, in the opinion of the Prime Minister, be required for the purpose of safeguarding national security, defence or public security.

In any proceedings in which the non-application of any provision of this Act on grounds of national security, defence or public security is in question, a certificate under the hand of the Prime Minister certifying that the non-application of the provision is required for the purpose of safeguarding national security, defence or public security shall be conclusive evidence of that fact.

sections.source_quote: No exception to this Act shall be allowed except where it constitutes a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society for – (a) the protection of national security, defence or public security; (b) the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of an offence, including the execution of a penalty; (c) an objective of general public interest, including an economic or financial interest of the State; (d) the protection of judicial independence and judicial proceedings; (e) the protection of a data subject or the rights and freedoms of others; or (f) issue of any licence, permit or authorisation during the COVID-19 period.

sections.heading: Annual report
sections.title: Annual reporting obligations for the Commissioner
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Commissioner must submit an annual report to the National Assembly within three months after the end of each fiscal year. The report includes details on the operation of Codes of Practice and Guidelines issued by the Commissioner, as well as any recommendations related to compliance with the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures transparency by requiring annual reporting on compliance activities, which could inform DTIA by highlighting regulatory oversight and the Commissioner's role in data protection.

requirements.text: The Commissioner shall, not later than 3 months after the end of every year, lay an annual report of the activities of the Office before the National Assembly.
The report shall include a statement about the operation of Codes of Practice issued or approved, or Guidelines issued, by the Commissioner.
The report shall include any recommendations that the Commissioner thinks fit, in relation to compliance with this Act.

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner shall, not later than 3 months after the end of every year, lay an annual report of the activities of the Office before the National Assembly.

sections.heading: Codes and Guidelines
sections.title: Codes and Guidelines for data protection
sections.summary: This section allows the Commissioner to issue or approve Codes of Practice and Guidelines to support compliance with the Act. It requires consultation with relevant parties before issuing any Code of Practice or Guidelines and permits variation or revocation of Codes of Practice, with approved Codes coming into effect on a specified date.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides mechanisms for regulatory guidance, which could influence DTIA by establishing standards for data processing activities and compliance.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may, for the purposes of this Act, issue or approve Codes of Practice, or issue Guidelines.
The Commissioner may, before issuing or approving a Code of Practice, or issuing Guidelines, consult such person or authority as he thinks fit.
Any Code of Practice may be varied or revoked.
Any Code of Practice shall, where it is approved under subsection (1), come into operation on a day specified by the Commissioner.

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner may, for the purposes of this Act, issue or approve Codes of Practice, or issue Guidelines.

sections.heading: Certification
sections.title: Data protection certification mechanisms
sections.summary: This section establishes voluntary data protection certification for controllers and processors, with certification valid for up to three years and renewable under the same conditions. Certification requires disclosure of processing activities to the Office and does not relieve the controller or processor of their compliance responsibilities under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section introduces certification as a compliance mechanism that could impact DTIA by providing a framework for data protection standards and third-party validation.

requirements.text: The Office may, in order to encourage compliance of processing operations by controllers and processors with this Act, lay down technical standards for data protection certification mechanisms and data protection seals and marks.
A certification shall be voluntary.
A certification shall be issued to a controller or processor for a maximum period of 3 years and may be renewed under the same conditions where the relevant requirements continue to be met.
A certification shall be withdrawn where the requirements for the certification are no longer met.

Where a controller or processor seeks certification under this section, he or it shall provide to the Office all information and access to his or its processing activities which are necessary to conduct the certification procedure.
A certification issued under this section shall not alter the responsibility of the controller or processor for compliance with this Act.

sections.source_quote: The Office may, in order to encourage compliance of processing operations by controllers and processors with this Act, lay down technical standards for data protection certification mechanisms and data protection seals and marks.

sections.heading: Confidentiality and oath
sections.title: Confidentiality and oath obligations
sections.summary: This section requires the Commissioner and authorized officers to take an oath as per the Schedule and prohibits disclosure of confidential information obtained during the exercise of powers under the Act. Violation of confidentiality can result in fines up to 50,000 rupees and imprisonment for up to two years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures confidentiality of sensitive data, which is critical for DTIA to maintain trust in data handling processes and prevent unauthorized disclosures.

requirements.text: The Commissioner and every authorised officer shall take the oath as set out in the Schedule.

No person who is or has been the Commissioner or an authorised officer shall, except in accordance with this Act or any other enactment, divulge any confidential information obtained in the exercise of a power or in the performance of a duty under this Act.

Any person who, without lawful excuse, contravenes subsection (2) shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding 50,000 rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 2 years.

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner and every authorised officer shall take the oath as set out in the Schedule.

sections.heading: Protection from liability
sections.title: Court immunity for Commissioner's actions
sections.summary: This section provides immunity from civil and criminal liability for the Commissioner or authorized officers acting within their duties under the Act. The Court may declare immunity if it finds reasonable cause for the action, and the Commissioner is immune from proceedings related to that action. This immunity applies to actions taken in good faith during the execution of duties.

requirements.text: The Court shall declare immunity for Commissioner or authorized officer actions taken in good faith with reasonable cause

sections.source_quote: Notwithstanding the Public Officers' Protection Act, where any action has been entered before a Court pursuant to an act done by the Commissioner or an authorised officer in the execution of his duties under this Act and it appears to the Court that there was reasonable cause to do such act, the Court shall so declare and thereafter the Commissioner or authorised officer shall be immune from all proceedings, whether civil or criminal, on account of such act.

sections.heading: Right of appeal
sections.title: Right to appeal decisions within 21 days
sections.summary: This section grants a right to appeal decisions made by the Commissioner within 21 days of becoming aware of the decision. The appeal must be filed with the Tribunal, and the appeal process is governed by specific timeframes and procedures outlined in this section.

requirements.text: Any person aggrieved by a Commissioner's decision may appeal to the Tribunal within 21 days of becoming aware of the decision

sections.source_quote: Any person aggrieved by a decision of the Commissioner under this Act may, within 21 days from the date when the decision is made known to that person, appeal to the Tribunal.

sections.heading: Special jurisdiction of Tribunal
sections.title: Tribunal's special jurisdiction for appeals
sections.summary: This section outlines the Tribunal's authority to hear appeals under the Act, including specific application of the Information and Communication Technologies Act, timeframes for appeal processing, and consequences for non-compliance with orders. The Tribunal must handle appeals expeditiously within six weeks and may allow late appeals with sufficient cause.

requirements.text: The Tribunal shall hear and dispose of appeals under this Act
Sections 40 to 44 of the Information and Communication Technologies Act apply to appeals under this Act
Sections 39 and 42(5) of the Information and Communication Technologies Act do not apply to appeals under this Act
Appeals must be filed in prescribed form and accompanied by prescribed fees
The Tribunal may entertain late appeals if satisfied there was sufficient cause for delay
The Tribunal may pass orders confirming, varying, or setting aside the decision appealed against after hearing parties
The Tribunal shall send copies of all orders to the parties
The Tribunal shall dispose of appeals within six weeks of lodgement
Non-compliance with Tribunal orders constitutes an offence
No appeal lies against decisions following settlements reached with consent of parties

sections.source_quote: Subject to subsections (2) and (3), the Tribunal shall hear and dispose of any appeal under this Act.

sections.heading: Prosecution and jurisdiction
sections.title: Prosecution and jurisdiction for offences
sections.summary: This section specifies that authorized officers may swear information for offences under the Act before a Magistrate, and the Intermediate Court has exclusive jurisdiction to try such offences. Prosecutions must be initiated by or with the consent of the Director of Public Prosecutions.

requirements.text: An authorized officer may swear an information for offences under this Act before a Magistrate
The Intermediate Court has jurisdiction to try offences under this Act
Prosecutions under this Act must be initiated by or with the consent of the Director of Public Prosecutions

sections.source_quote: An authorised officer may swear an information in respect of an offence under this Act or any regulations made under it before a Magistrate.

sections.heading: Certificate issued by Commissioner
sections.title: Commissioner's certified copies and certificates
sections.summary: This section establishes that copies of entries in the register certified by the Commissioner are admissible evidence, and certificates signed by the Commissioner stating the absence of an entry for a specified person are also admissible evidence.

requirements.text: A copy of, or extract from, an entry in the register certified by the Commissioner is evidence of the entry
A certificate signed by the Commissioner stating no entry exists for a specified person is evidence of that fact

sections.source_quote: a copy of, or of an extract from, an entry in the register duly certified by the Commissioner to be a true copy shall be evidence of the entry or extract; and a certificate signed by the Commissioner and stating that there is not an entry in the register in respect of a specified person as a controller or processor shall be evidence of that fact.

sections.heading: Regulations
sections.title: Regulations for the Act
sections.summary: This section allows the Minister to make regulations for the purposes of the Act after consulting with the Commissioner. Regulations may cover amendments to the Schedule, requirements for controllers and processors, notice and registration contents, information to data subjects, fees, Codes of Practice, and penalties for contraventions.

requirements.text: The Minister may make regulations for the purposes of this Act after consulting with the Commissioner
Regulations may provide for amendment of the Schedule
Regulations may specify requirements for controllers or processors when processing personal data
Regulations may define contents for notices or registrations by controllers or processors
Regulations may specify information to be provided to data subjects and how it shall be provided
Regulations may provide for levying fees and taking charges
Regulations may issue and approve Codes of Practice and Guidelines
Regulations may provide that contraventions constitute offences punishable by fines up to 100,000 rupees and imprisonment up to 5 years

sections.source_quote: The Minister may, for the purposes of this Act, after consultation with the Commissioner, make such regulations as he thinks fit.

sections.heading: Repeal
sections.title: Repeal of Data Protection Act
sections.summary: This section explicitly repeals the Data Protection Act. It states that the repealed Act is no longer in force and its provisions are replaced by the current Act. The repeal is effective from the commencement of the current Act.

requirements.text: The Data Protection Act is repealed.

sections.source_quote: 56. Repeal The Data Protection Act is repealed.

sections.heading: Transitional provisions
sections.title: Transitional provisions for Data Protection Office assets
sections.summary: This section outlines transitional arrangements for assets, rights, and obligations related to the Data Protection Office under the repealed Act. It ensures continuity of assets, rights, and liabilities, and transfers the register of controllers and processors to the new system. Pending applications and codes of practice continue to be valid under the new Act.

requirements.text: All assets and funds of the Data Protection Office under the repealed Act shall remain vested in the Office.
All rights, obligations and liabilities subsisting in favour of or against the Data Protection Office under the repealed Act shall continue to exist under the same terms and conditions.
The contents of the register of controllers and processors kept under the repealed Act shall be transferred to the register kept under this Act.
Any registration granted under the repealed Act, which is valid at the commencement of this Act, shall be deemed to have been granted under this Act.
Any application made to the Data Protection Office under the repealed Act and which is pending at the commencement of this Act shall be dealt with in accordance with this Act.
Any Code of Practice or Guideline issued under the repealed Act shall continue to remain in force.
Any act or thing done, or any contract or agreement entered into by the Data Protection Office shall be deemed to have been done or entered into by the Office.

All proceedings, judicial or otherwise, initiated before and pending at the commencement of this Act, by or against the Data Protection Office or the Commissioner, may be continued by or against the Office or the Commissioner.

For the purpose of section 45, the period starting from the commencement of this Act to the end of the year of such commencement shall be deemed to be the first year for the filing of the annual report.
Where this Act does not make provision for any transition, the Minister may make such regulations as may be necessary for such transition.

sections.source_quote: 57. Transitional provisions (1) In this section – "repealed Act" means the Data Protection Act. (2) All assets and funds of the Data Protection Office under the repealed Act shall, at the commencement of this Act, remain vested in the Office. (3) All rights, obligations and liabilities subsisting in favour of or against the Data Protection Office under the repealed Act shall, at the commencement of this Act, continue to exist under the same terms and conditions in favour of or against the Office. (4) The contents of the register of controllers and processors kept under the repealed Act shall, at the commencement of this Act, be transferred to the register kept under this Act. (5) Any registration granted under the repealed Act, which is valid at the commencement of this Act, shall be deemed to have been granted under this Act. (6) Any application made to the Data Protection Office under the repealed Act and which is pending at the commencement of this Act shall be dealt with in accordance with this Act. (7) Any Code of Practice or Guideline issued under the repealed Act shall, at the commencement of this Act, continue to remain in force. (8) Any act or thing done, or any contract or agreement entered into, by the Data Protection Office shall, at the commencement of this Act, be deemed to have been done or entered into by the Office. (9) All proceedings, judicial or otherwise, initiated before and pending at the commencement of this Act, by or against the Data Protection Office or the Commissioner, may be continued, by or against the Office or the Commissioner, as the case may be. (10) For the purpose of section 45, the period starting from the commencement of this Act to the end of the year of such commencement shall be deemed to be the first year for the filing of the annual report. (11) Where this Act does not make provision for any transition, the Minister may make such regulations as may be necessary for such transition.

sections.heading: Commencement
sections.title: Commencement date for the Act
sections.summary: This section specifies the commencement date of the Act, stating that it comes into operation on a date to be fixed by Proclamation, with the possibility of different dates for different sections. The Act was proclaimed by a specific Proclamation in 2018, effective from 15 January 2018.

requirements.text: This Act shall come into operation on a date to be fixed by Proclamation.
Different dates may be fixed for the coming into operation of different sections of this Act.

sections.source_quote: 58. Commencement Proclaimed by [Proclamation of 2018] w.e.f. 15 January 2018 (1) Subject to subsection (2), this Act shall come into operation on a date to be fixed by Proclamation. (2) Different dates may be fixed for the coming into operation of different sections of this Act.
